topic: Context 3: School Life, Studies and the World of Work
subject: french

items.q: How do you say 'I study maths' in French?
items.why: Students drop the article like English. School subjects in French take the DEFINITE article: 'j'aime le français', 'j'étudie les sciences', 'je déteste l'histoire'. This is the rule for general nouns.

items.q: Which verbs take 'être' as their auxiliary in the passé composé?
items.correct: Movement/state-change verbs (DR & MRS VAN DER TRAMP) and all reflexive verbs.
items.why: Students forget reflexives. Both groups use être: aller/venir/monter, etc. AND every reflexive verb (je me suis lavé). With être, the past participle agrees with the subject.

items.q: How do you say 'I am eating bread' (some bread) in French?
items.random_wrong: Je mange le pain (which means 'the bread').
items.why: Students drop the partitive. French requires DU/DE LA/DES for 'some' even when English omits it. Skipping it makes the sentence ungrammatical in French.

items.q: Which sentence means 'Maths is more difficult than English'?
items.correct: Les maths sont plus difficiles que l'anglais.
items.misconception: Les maths est plus difficile que l'anglais.
items.random_wrong: Les maths sont moins difficiles que l'anglais.
items.why: Correct answer: Les maths sont plus difficiles que l'anglais.. “Les maths est plus difficile que l'anglais.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's les devoirs. “la récréation” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many lessons typically make up a French collège school day (matin + après-midi, approximate standard)?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 lessons. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
